title: 2. Conventional Process / Existing Gaps
description:
2. Conventional Process / Existing Gaps
Sample flow measured manually using rotameters
No real-time feedback, alarm, or interlock for low-flow conditions
Flow degradation detected only after analyzer trip
Reactive troubleshooting instead of preventive action
RCFA shows repeated flow-related and probe choking failures 3. Problem Statement
Drop in sample flow below threshold leads to analyzer trips causing:
LD gas recovery miss
BF PCI injection interruption
CDQ bunkering interruption
Blowing miss and blow heat miss
       
        FY-25 impact :

6 LD blowing misses, 
4 BF PCI interruptions, 
2 blow heat misses →     ₹1.9 Cr 
loss due to unreliable SHS operation. 4. Proposed Solutions (RCFA-Based)
    Dual sample probe with manual changeover → Ensures uninterrupted sampling during probe choking or failure
    Hot redundancy for SHS power & critical modules → Instantaneous switchover without analyzer shutdown
    Primary Solution: Real-Time Gas Flow Monitoring Manual Flow Element Signal Conditioning & Display 4–20 mA Output Analyzer PLC Rotational Speed Flow Sensor SHS Reliability Enhancement Using a Rotameter Monitoring & Alert Generation System TECH EX 2026 : (TE-26/037) 1. Introduction
The Sample Handling System (SHS) conditions raw process gas by controlling temperature, moisture, pressure, and flow before analysis. Reliable SHS operation is critical for automation, interlocking, and gas recovery across BF, LD, Coke Plant, and Mills. Any instability directly impacts analyzer availability and process continuity. 5. Proposed Solution – System Concept Raw Process Gas 6. Technical Highlights
Series-mounted rotational speed–based flow sensing element
Signal linearized and converted to standard 4–20 mA
Plug-and-play integration with existing PLC
Enables alarms, trends, and future data logging
No major SHS modification required Fig. Block Diagram 7. Challenges Addressed
Undetected flow degradation
Delayed choke identification
Analyzer trips due to blind operation
Repeated flow-related SHS failures 8. Benefits
Real-time flow visibility and early warning
Reduction in analyzer trips and process interruptions
Preventive maintenance instead of reactive action
Improved SHS availability and process reliability
Digital flow meter functionality at ~25–30% cost 9. Uniqueness / Novelty
Cost-optimized alternative to digital flow meters
Converts a manual, open-loop system into a monitored closed-loop system
Scalable across multiple SHS points with minimal CAPEX
Directly derived from RCFA, Pareto, and trend analysis 10. Way Forward
Pilot implementation on critical SHS points
PLC-based alarm optimization and trip logic
Long-term trend analysis for predictive choking detection
Plant-wide replication across BF, LD, and Mills Img. CDI 5100 Inline flowmeter Cost per unit of Solution Total Cost Incurred 61,600/- 3.58 Cr/- Savings
= 12.1 Cr/-

title: Introduction
description:
Introduction
Tata Steel lacks a dynamic, sensor-driven system to estimate the Remaining Useful Life (RUL) of motors. As a result, both unexpected motor failures and premature maintenance occur, leading to significant downtime, increased maintenance cost, and suboptimal asset utilization. There is a critical need for a real-time RUL prediction framework that continuously monitors motor degradation, forecasts failure proactively, and optimizes maintenance timing. Proposed System
A sensor-integrated predictive maintenance framework that continuously monitors motor health and predicts time-to-failure.
Sensors Used: Vibration, temperature, current, and RPM.
Process Flow:
Real-time sensor data acquisition.
ML model analysis for degradation pattern detection.
RUL estimation and live dashboard visualization.
Prototype: Small-scale motor setup with live dashboard for real-time condition monitoring and predictive insights. Benefits
Reduces unplanned downtime and maintenance costs.
Improves equipment reliability and operational efficiency.
Provides a scalable framework for industrial predictive maintenance. Business Potential
Applicable across Tata Steel units and other industries using rotating machinery.
Reduces unexpected production halts and increases asset life.
Supports transition toward Industry 4.0 predictive maintenance systems. Way Forward
Develop and test a pilot prototype in selected plant sections.
Dashboard for real-time alerts.
Collaborate with maintenance teams for implementation feedback and training. Challenges
Sensor calibration and noise interference in harsh industrial settings.
Limited initial dataset for ML model training.
Integration with existing IT  systems.
Ensuring reliability and accuracy during live operations.

title: Center Alignment Mechanism For Oxygen Lance in LD Converter Introduction:
description:
Center Alignment Mechanism For Oxygen Lance in LD Converter Introduction:
LD#1 converters face frequent downtime and hot metal loss due to improper center alignment of the oxygen lance during changeover and maintenance. Accurate center lance alignment is essential for stable blowing, to avoid tip burn/puncture and associated vessel incidents, and to reduce unplanned stoppages. The proposed concept introduces a simple, laser-based reference system that makes lance alignment fast, repeatable and largely operator‑independent. Benefits & Business Potential:
Alignment time reduced from 52 min to 22 min (avg). 
Lance‑related downtime expected to reduce by 40–50%. 
Recovery of 15–20 heats/year. 
Hot metal throughput recovery ~2,775–3,700 t/year. 
Low‑cost retrofit using standard laser modules. Uniqueness of the approach:
Converts subjective, operator‑based alignment into a clear, reference‑driven process. 
Uses simple laser-sheet hardware with magnetic fixture.
Portable setup usable across multiple vessels and lances in LD#1. Challenges:
RCFA FY25 shows ~2,509 minutes/year of lance‑related downtime. 
Production loss equivalent to ~38.5 heats/year. 
Hot metal loss = 38.5 heats × 160 t/heat = ~6,160 t/year. 
Misalignment increases risk of tip burn/puncture. 
Adds mechanical stress on rope, gearbox, and carriage systems. Proposed System: Laser-based center lance alignment :
Install vertical laser sheets near the lance handling area to create a fixed reference plane.  
Mount a laser alignment target/plate on the lance and use a plumb (or equivalent) to validate verticality.  
Adjust lance position until its axis coincides with the laser plane at predefined reference marks.  
Use magnetic / clamped fixtures for laser modules to allow quick mounting and removal. 
Standardized SOP converts the activity into a quantitative, reference‑based process with minimal trials. Conventional Process:
Alignment is done by trial‑and‑error from the operating platform.  
Operators rely on visual judgement and repeated traversing of the lance carriage.  
Typical alignment cycle: 50–55 minutes per lance change (average ~52 min). Reference Plain

title: Dehumidification of Cold Blast Air from Blower to Blast Furnace . When moisture in the hot blast air increases, it creates several problems:
description:
Dehumidification of Cold Blast Air from Blower to Blast Furnace . When moisture in the hot blast air increases, it creates several problems:
Decreases blast furnace stability and production.
Increases coke rate consumption and decreases PCI.
Increases blower power and stove power consumption.
Increases corrosion of ducts and other parts. 2026 Coke cost savings up to ₹42 crores and lowering the coke rate by 2%.
An increase in production by 1%, resulting in an annual profit gain of up to ₹10.5 crores.
Decreased energy consumption in blower house by 9.4% saving up to ₹10.7 crores.
Producing 48 lakh gallons of distilled water worth ₹ 8.73 crores.
Higher usage rate of PCI.
Increased stability of Blast furnace.
Total Value generated up to ₹68 crores/annum Introduction In case of filter failure, risk of dust and condensate forming sludge, mitigated by optimized fin spacing and in-situ washing.
Risk of added pressure drop affecting blower stability, mitigated by low-velocity design and auto-bypass dampers. Challenges Benefits Proposed Solution The system uses a reverse Carnot refrigeration cycle to cool air to its dew point, condensing water vapor into liquid.   
A 40KV Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) ionizes and captures any residual micro water droplets splashed into the air.  
Arduino-controlled dampers ensure the synchronized opening and closing of the dehumidifying and bypass ducts to prevent pressure changes.  
Copper coils with blue aluminum TiO_2 coated fins and precooling tubes are used to reach an absolute humidity of 10g/Nm^3.  
The layout includes a bypass duct and monitoring probes to maintain blast furnace stability during unit maintenance or startup. Perform CFD and thermal modelling to optimize airflow, heat transfer and overall efficiency. 
Explore advanced surface coatings to improve condensation and reduce fouling. Way Forward Dehumidification of Cold Blast Air from Blower to Blast Furnace When moisture in the hot blast air increases, it creates several problems:
Decreases blast furnace stability and production.
Increases coke rate consumption and decreases PCI.
Increases blower power and stove power consumption.
Increases corrosion of ducts and other parts. 2026 Coke cost savings up to ₹42 crores and lowering the coke rate by 2%.
An increase in production by 1%, resulting in an annual profit gain of up to ₹10.5 crores.
Decreased energy consumption in blower house by 9.4% saving up to ₹10.7 crores.
Producing 48 lakh gallons of distilled water worth ₹ 8.73 crores.
Higher usage rate of PCI.
Increased stability of Blast furnace.
Total Value generated up to ₹68 crores/annum Introduction In case of filter failure, risk of dust and condensate forming sludge, mitigated by optimized fin spacing and in-situ washing.
Risk of added pressure drop affecting blower stability, mitigated by low-velocity design and auto-bypass dampers. Challenges Benefits Proposed Solution The system uses a reverse Carnot refrigeration cycle to cool air to its dew point, condensing water vapor into liquid.   
A 40KV Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP) ionizes and captures any residual micro water droplets splashed into the air.  
Arduino-controlled dampers ensure the synchronized opening and closing of the dehumidifying and bypass ducts to prevent pressure changes.  
Copper coils with blue aluminium fins are used to attain an absolute humidity of 10g/Nm^3.  
The layout includes a bypass duct and monitoring probes to maintain blast furnace stability during unit maintenance or startup. Perform CFD and thermal modelling to optimize airflow, heat transfer and overall efficiency. 
Explore advanced surface coatings to improve condensation and reduce fouling. Way Forward Uniqueness Humidity sensor-controlled flow valve dynamically adjusts cooling based on relative humidity, optimizing energy consumption.
This design is compact and adaptable, fitting space constraints without the bulk of traditional desiccator wheels.

title: PREDICTIVE DETECTION OF SEGMENT ROLL STANDSTILL TO PREVENT SLABSURFACE DEFECTS IN CONTINUOUS CASTING . 2026 Introduction Benefits Uniqueness Conventional Process Block Diagram Proposed Solution Deploy across all caster segments.
description:
PREDICTIVE DETECTION OF SEGMENT ROLL STANDSTILL TO PREVENT SLABSURFACE DEFECTS IN CONTINUOUS CASTING . 2026 Introduction Benefits Uniqueness Conventional Process Block Diagram Proposed Solution Deploy across all caster segments.
Integrate with PLC + central dashboard.
Real Time Monitoring in HMI.
Standardize & expand plantwide. Way Forward Continuous casting uses~15 segments, 20 rolls each, ~300 non driven rolls/caster.
Non driven rolls guide slabs but can jam due to wear or contamination
Jammed rolls risk marks, quality loss & downtime Analysis shows roll jamming accounts for ~84% of slabs held. Jamming detected only after groove marks appear. 
No roll‑wise visibility → complete segment must be opened.
 Minimum 4–8 hours downtime → ₹2.8–₹5.7 crore loss First system providing roll‑wise real-time visibility. 
Rugged sensors suited for high heat, water & EMI.
 Low-cost, passive, reliable solution. Magnetic reed sensor + magnet on each roll. 
Real-time speed & slip detection. 
PLC integration → alerts for exact roll number. 
Predictive maintenance instead of reactive. 70–80% reduction in roll-related defects.
Prevents groove marks, partial cooling, surface scratches. 
Major reduction in downtime cost (₹72 lakhs /hr).
 Targeted maintenance → open the segment selectively Roll jam effect: Stationary roll scratches slab → groove marks

title: Monitoring Internal Leakages in Hydraulic Cylinders of HSM . Hydraulic cylinders are crucial for the precise operation of  Hot Strip Mill.
description:
Monitoring Internal Leakages in Hydraulic Cylinders of HSM . Hydraulic cylinders are crucial for the precise operation of  Hot Strip Mill.
Internal leakage, a hidden fault, severely impacts performance and can lead to costly failures.
Current detection methods are often reactive, identifying issues mainly after significant problems arise.
This project introduces a proactive monitoring approach to ensure enhanced reliability and efficiency. 2026 Early and accurate detection of internal leakage prevents unexpected failures and ensures precise control in HSM operations.
It significantly improves the reliability and stability of the hot strip mill, reducing unplanned downtime and operational disruptions.
It enables predictive, data-driven maintenance, leading to substantial cost savings by avoiding production losses and optimizing asset lifespan. Introduction Installation of sensors can be tricky  as we are dealing with various kinds of cylinders.
Scaling the project  poses a significant financial hurdle, with approximately 990 hydraulic cylinders in HSM and 50,000 actuators across Tata Steel needing monitoring.
Managing data from a vast sensor network across multiple locations like TSJ, TSM, and TSK presents logistical and data integration challenges. Challenges Benefits It uniquely uses sound patterns from internal oil flow to diagnose seal health, offering a non-traditional and non-invasive approach.
The system identifies the onset of leakage long before performance degradation becomes evident, unlike conventional visual or thermal methods.
This transforms maintenance from reactive repairs to a data-driven, predictive strategy, preventing costly unexpected failures and providing objective insights into a hidden fault. Uniqueness It primarily relies on  detection methods like visual inspection, thermography and use of ultraprobe.
These methods are mostly reactive, identifying issues only after significant performance degradation.
This means problems are often caught late, leading to unplanned downtime and efficiency losses. Conventional Process Proposed Solution Acoustic sensors are installed on hydraulic cylinders to capture sound signatures of oil flow.
These signals are analyzed to identify distinct acoustic wave patterns corresponding to different  conditions.
This enables early, predictive detection of internal leakage, shifting from reactive to predictive maintenance. Installation and standardization of process at HSM.
Installation at other shopfloors which heavily depend on hydraulic cylinders. Way Forward Block Diagram Introduction Conventional Process

title: Design & Development of Inspection Riveting Marking Machine for Medium & Large Ball Bearing . The manufacturing process of bearings begins with the careful selection of raw materials and progresses through various stages of machining, heat treatment, assembly and final inspection.
description:
Design & Development of Inspection Riveting Marking Machine for Medium & Large Ball Bearing . The manufacturing process of bearings begins with the careful selection of raw materials and progresses through various stages of machining, heat treatment, assembly and final inspection. 
Bearing manufacturers are increasingly incorporating advanced technologies, robotic automation, and digital inspection systems to improve efficiency, precision, and quality control in the manufacturing process.
At Tata Bearings, the inspection riveting marking(IRM) machine is a specialized, integrated system that performs three critical final assembly and quality functions in a single automated cell.
Inspection (Post-Assembly Quality Check)
Riveting (Cage Fastening)
Marking (Traceability & Branding) 2026 Increased productivity (Setting time reduced) and efficiency (Aligned with the Grinding module).


Optimized resource utilization Introduction Benefits Cost savings through in-house design and manufacturing. Uniqueness Proposed System In house design, develop and implement a new IRM machine for the Medium and Large ball bearing(MLBB) in assembly section.

Increasing MLBB bearing production to meet market demand effectively. The operation is carried out manually, so  exploring potential for further automation to eliminate the Man-machine interface.

To develop IRM machine for small Ball Bearing for outer dia. up to 62 mm. Way Forward

title: Elimination of Human Machine Intervention in Sick Wagon Detection at Rapid Loading System (RLS) Logistics Noamundi equipped with three Rapid Loading System (RLS) having a capability of 24 MTPA, dispatches Iron ore to different customers viz., TSJ, TSK, TSG.
description:
Elimination of Human Machine Intervention in Sick Wagon Detection at Rapid Loading System (RLS) Logistics Noamundi equipped with three Rapid Loading System (RLS) having a capability of 24 MTPA, dispatches Iron ore to different customers viz., TSJ, TSK, TSG. 
Two types of rakes – BOBSN & BOXN are used here for loading. Rakes are back pushed from exchange yard to RLS. 
After back pushing, wagon checking and fitting activities are being carried out before loading for identification of defective/sick wagons and unwanted/foreign materials. 2026 Background Benefits Earlier Method Wagon checking for detection of sick wagons & identification of unwanted materials were being done manually before loading. 
After identification of sick wagon, the person used to climb over the rake & fix a red flag, which demarcates that the wagon is sick & no loading must be done in it. 
The list of such wagons are then communicated to Railways for further verification. 
However, this process, poses a continuous risk of slip, trip & fall hazards.  Also, continuous loco movement poses a fatal potential hazard for the working crew. Implemented Method No manpower required for identification of sick wagon leading to reduction of slip, trip & fall hazards.
System optimization resulted in increased efficiency in rake checking and fitting activities. An AI camera-based system was installed at RLS rake entry gate, which automatically detects the defective wagon and the foreign materials inside the wagons. (ref. Fig. 1)
After detection, it generates a report of wagon numbers with pictures, which is further checked by RLS control room & sent to Railways for verification. (ref. Fig. 2)
The wagon inspection report generated by AI system is validated during wagon checking & the sick wagon numbers are entered in PLC.
As the sick wagon enters the loading zone, pop up with hooter is generated in PLC for sick wagon & alerts the control room operator.  (ref. Fig. 3) Fig. 1 Fig. 2 Fig. 3

title: Passive Zero-Energy Karakuri Safety System for Automatic Pit Isolation in Down ender Operation. In the packing line at Tata Bluescope, the down ender tilts coils to a vertical position, exposing a dip pit that creates a high-risk man-fall hazard during operation.
description:
Passive Zero-Energy Karakuri Safety System for Automatic Pit Isolation in Down ender Operation. In the packing line at Tata Bluescope, the down ender tilts coils to a vertical position, exposing a dip pit that creates a high-risk man-fall hazard during operation. 

As operators work near the pit for strapping, an inherently safe, automatic pit protection system is required. 2026 Background Eliminates man–machine interaction at a high-risk pit zone
Zero energy usage with no operating cost
High reliability through simple mechanical design
Low maintenance and long service life Benefits Implemented Solution The proposed solution is a purely mechanical, zero-energy Karakuri safety gate, interlocked with the downender through a rope–pulley mechanism.

The gate automatically closes before pit exposure as the downender tilts and reopens when the coils return to vertical.

The system is inherently fail-safe, with the gate defaulting to the closed position. Block Diagram Cost Constraint: Designed with minimal budget using in-house resources.
In-House Development: Concept, design, and execution driven entirely by internal innovation. Challenges Business Potential This solution can be standardized and replicated across downenders and coil-handling pits, delivering a scalable safety upgrade. 

It significantly reduces accident risk without adding energy consumption, automation complexity, or operating costs—making it a high-impact, low-cost safety intervention.
. Earlier Condition Post project implementation

title: Reduction of Suspended Particulate Matter (SPM) Emissions from Coke Oven Battery The NINL Coke Oven Battery, commissioned in 2004, underwent a shutdown in 2020 without prior mitigation measures.
description:
Reduction of Suspended Particulate Matter (SPM) Emissions from Coke Oven Battery The NINL Coke Oven Battery, commissioned in 2004, underwent a shutdown in 2020 without prior mitigation measures. 
It was recommissioned in September 2023 following repairs to 4-6 flues. Currently, elevated SPM emissions are observed from the chimney due to aging and operational constraints. 
This project aims to analyze the SPM carryover mechanism and implement feasible mitigation measures without impacting production. 2026 Background Benefits & Business Potential Problem Statement Ageing battery with prolonged thermal deterioration.

Leading to cross-leakages between carbonizing chambers and heating flues.

Fine dust particles carried along with waste flue gas and travels through the flue tunnel to the chimney

Resulting in elevated SPM values (Average SPM ~55 mg/Nm3 and frequent SPM alerts (~27 per day) at the stack. Way Forward Methodology adopted Ageing battery with limited scope for major refractory repair during operation.
Cross-leakages cannot be eliminated without extended shutdown. Challenges Representative schematic for understanding SPM carryover mechanism Implemented Solution:

Detailed study of SPM generation and dust carryover mechanism.
Evaluation of practical emission reduction options suitable for ageing battery. 
Selection of dust suppression approach without structural chimney modification.
Design and installation of Water Spray Dust Suppression System inside chimney. Significant reduction in SPM alerts (30-40 % reduction in stack alerts)
Improved environmental compliance. Exploring zero emissions with additional Measures :- 
Regular dusting and ceramic welding at leakage points.
Flue grouting to minimize cross-leakages.
Daily spray flushing & nozzle inspection. Photos captured during system installation and commissioning

title: There are four bins in Jig each of capacity 250 T. When Jig needs to be operated, Fines from wash plant is diverted towards jig and filled in bins with help of FC9 Conveyor which is a shuttle conveyor. Once one bin is filled the conveyor needs to be shifted. This operation is operator based.  If operator is not vigilant, the bins overflow, jamming the conveyor and causing detention.
description:
There are four bins in Jig each of capacity 250 T. When Jig needs to be operated, Fines from wash plant is diverted towards jig and filled in bins with help of FC9 Conveyor which is a shuttle conveyor. Once one bin is filled the conveyor needs to be shifted. This operation is operator based.  If operator is not vigilant, the bins overflow, jamming the conveyor and causing detention. 
In order to eliminate the operator-based operation of the conveyor, we will automate the process in such a way that the logic shall automatically shift the conveyor based on bin levels. FC9 is a conveyor that fills the four Jig Bins. The conveyor supports both reversible and traverse motion. The bins are clubbed as Bin 1 & 3 and Bin 2 & 4. When conveyor is placed at any one of the club, the conveyor is reversed to fill the next bin of the club and then the conveyor is shifted to another bin club. The whole process is man (operator) dependent who operates it via SCADA. If the operator is not vigilant then bins fill and overflow, jamming the conveyor and its movement. Analysis Results Counter Measures Installation of chute level sensors

Logic development and implementation so that if for a pair the bin level goes beyond 90% conveyor reverses

If both the bins of a pair bin fill, the conveyor shifts to another pair.

If all the bin level goes beyond 90%, the feeding circuit stops and alarm is triggered. REDUCTION OF MANUAL INTERVENTION BY AUTO SHIFTING OF FC#9 SHUTTLE CONVEYOR BASED ON BIN LEVEL AT JIG PLANT Abhinav Kumar Sah : Manager Jig & Hydrocyclone , PP1, NIM

title: Learning & Development Tech Ex-2026 …....Project Presentation for Screening (Sample) INTRODUCTION VCB/MOCB/VI CLOSING & TRIPPING TEST JIG •VCB is a three phase HT switch. During its maintenance, trial and testing is needed several times. Closing and tripping actions are done outside of its panel by opening live HT panel control power (110VDC).
description:
Learning & Development Tech Ex-2026 …....Project Presentation for Screening (Sample) INTRODUCTION VCB/MOCB/VI CLOSING & TRIPPING TEST JIG •VCB is a three phase HT switch. During its maintenance, trial and testing is needed several times. Closing and tripping actions are done outside of its panel by opening live HT panel control power (110VDC).
• This test zig provides stand alone power supply for safe operation of the VCB/MOCB outside of its panel. Proposed system Proposed VCB testing system Jig is made of PVC for shock free and light weight. It is a portable power supply system.
This test zig consists of 2 MCB one for main incoming supply and other for spring charging of VCB.
 2 indication lights, one for incoming supply ON and other for system ready to operate.
2 push buttons for closing and tripping of VCB.
By using this test jig now VCB testing can be done without opening live HT panel. Benefits Management always motivates us to work safely; therefore this test jig minimizes high risk and unsafe work. Thus, making the process safe.
This is a portable system that allows us to conduct VCB testing anywhere, there is no longer a need for any additional external special power source to perform these tests. Business potential This idea can be horizontally deployed across all location where VCB testing is performed. Way forward By implementing this idea, we can either establish our own test jig manufacturing plant or sell the patent to an existing Test Jig manufacturing company. Challenges:
Our primary challenge was to eliminate unsafe practices and transit to a safe process. Working on HT live panels is extremely hazardous and carries a high risk of fatal accidents; therefore, ensuring safety was our biggest priority. BEFORE AFTER TEST JIG

title: Introduction Design, Development & Manufacturing of Dewatering Drum for I-Blast Furnace Dewatering rotating Drum is a key component in a slag granulation system that separates granulated blast furnace slag from water. It efficiently dewaters the slag, converting it into a valuable, dry product for the industry, while enabling 100% water recycling and reducing environmental impact. Previous Practice Way Forward Proposed Solution Challenges Proprietary item Procured from OEMs. In-house Design , Development & Manufacturing of Dewatering rotating Drum (In TGS  Manufacturing facility) Design- OEMs design & Detail drawings not available.
description:

It efficiently dewaters the slag, converting it into a valuable, dry product for the industry, while enabling 100% water recycling and reducing environmental impact. Previous Practice Way Forward Proposed Solution Challenges Proprietary item Procured from OEMs. In-house Design , Development & Manufacturing of Dewatering rotating Drum (In TGS  Manufacturing facility) Design- OEMs design & Detail drawings not available.

2. Cycle Time Optimization.
3. Cost Optimization – Manufacturing
4. Cost Optimization - Sourcing. Benefits Capability Enhancement
Reduced dependency on OEMs for timely delivery

Spares availability.

title: Digital Transformation Of Coil Image Capturing And Coil Data Validation The project was undertaken to improve the efficiency, accuracy, and reliability of coil image capturing, inspection, and labeling processes across Station-3 and Station-14 at JCAPCPL.
description:
Digital Transformation Of Coil Image Capturing And Coil Data Validation The project was undertaken to improve the efficiency, accuracy, and reliability of coil image capturing, inspection, and labeling processes across Station-3 and Station-14 at JCAPCPL.
These activities were performed manually earlier, resulting in delays, inconsistencies, and higher dependency on manpower. 2026 Background Improved accuracy and consistency in coil inspection.
Faster image capturing and label scanning.
Enhanced traceability and digital record management.
Reduction in human error and operator workload. Benefits Implemented Solution Automated Image Capturing System – Cameras installed at Station-3 and Station-14 capture coil images automatically.


Integrated QR Code Reader – Automated scanning ensures accuracy and eliminates manual errors.


Automated Coil data validation Workflow – End-to-end process is automated, reducing human intervention.


Reduced Man–Machine Interaction – Automation eliminates repetitive manual tasks, improving safety and productivity. Block Diagram The primary challenges stem from the unpredictable industrial environment (complex & variable lighting), the physical variability of the coils, and the technical limitations of vision systems. Challenges Business Potential Horizontal deployment in Coil Packaging Line at various locations. Coil feeding in CPL Auto rotation, auto QR code scanning & auto photo capturing Coil packaging as per customer specification Auto QR code scanning & auto photo capturing FG label pasting on the packed coil JIRITSUKA Method Manual Image Capturing & Label Scanning The automatic coil data validation and auto photo capturing system can be integrated with Industry 4.0 technologies such as machine vision, deep learning, and robust data management systems into the production line. This will create a closed-loop system for real-time quality control and traceability. Way Forward

title: Conveyor component failures cause belt damage, downtime & production loss.
description:
Conveyor component failures cause belt damage, downtime & production loss.
Manual inspection is slow & subjective; early faults often go unnoticed.
Wear of idlers and pulleys produces unique acoustic patterns detectable via sensors.
Fixed microphones enable automated health monitoring. Microphones placed along conveyors capture idler sound continuously.
Local Processing Units (LPUs) analyze signals using AI/ML to detect anomalies.
Fault insights sent wirelessly to control room dashboard for maintenance alerts.
Operators can review audio logs & plan preventive maintenance. For large-scale deployment, the analysis framework presently in use, can be adapted to Distributed Optical Fiber Sensing (DOFS), enabling cost-effective continuous monitoring across entire conveyor lengths Sensors need dust/weather protection & periodic cleaning.
Varying load affects acoustic patterns, requires model calibration.
Integration with existing plant systems & networks needed 2026
an Exhibition of Engineering Prowess

TE-026/085 Undetected conveyor component failures lead to unplanned stoppages & disruptions in scheduled production. Problem Statement Introduction Proposed System Smart Conveyor Inspection System for Predictive Maintenance Block Diagram Way Forward Key Results Achieved 88% classification accuracy, 100% fault detection, 0% false alarms during testing. Challenges Deployable on critical conveyors across mines & material handling units.
Modular & scalable architecture for plant-wide implementation.
Aligns with Tata Steel’s digital & predictive maintenance goals. Business Potential Detects faults early, preventing belt & pulley damage.
Enables continuous, real-time predictive monitoring.
Reduces manual inspections, false alarms & maintenance costs.
Provides objective, data-driven maintenance decisions. Benefits

title: 1 REDUCTION OF IDLE FREIGHT USING OLWMS IN RLS Introduction
description:
1 REDUCTION OF IDLE FREIGHT USING OLWMS IN RLS Introduction
Despite accurate loading within a 1% tolerance band, the rake consistently departs with total chargeable weight (actual mass discharged by the RLS gates) below the government‑permitted capacity, causing idle freight and cost inefficiency. Benefits
Reduction in Idle Freight cost
Higher rake capacity utilization
Improved operational efficiency and higher turnaround
Reduced number of rake trips annually for the same tonnage
Lower carbon footprint per ton-km Proposed System
Implement an operator-in-loop dynamic loading adjustment system with a real-time dashboard that monitors cumulative weight and alerts the operator to redistribute residual tonnage across remaining wagons by adjusting gate open time. Cost Considerations

title: Highwall mining extracts only 30–37% of coal due to stability requirements. Paste backfilling can increase recovery to 80–85%, ensuring stability and sustainability Introduction • Tracking the paste solidification time•  How to control the flow of the paste along the dip.• Barricading strength and leakage prevention.• Limited to only 40m  OB strata length using conventional drilling techniques Challenges Proposed System Fly ash + OB mixed with cement and water to create paste. Pumped through vertical boreholes into highwall voids. Barricading at gallery mouth using OB rock to ensure paste confinement. Enables higher percentage of coal extraction from highwall area.
description:
Highwall mining extracts only 30–37% of coal due to stability requirements. Paste backfilling can increase recovery to 80–85%, ensuring stability and sustainability Introduction • Tracking the paste solidification time•  How to control the flow of the paste along the dip.• Barricading strength and leakage prevention.• Limited to only 40m  OB strata length using conventional drilling techniques Challenges Proposed System Fly ash + OB mixed with cement and water to create paste. Pumped through vertical boreholes into highwall voids. Barricading at gallery mouth using OB rock to ensure paste confinement. Enables higher percentage of coal extraction from highwall area.
Stabilizes highwall and prevents subsidence.
Utilizes fly ash and OB, reducing waste dumping
Increased profit margin in Highwall mining sector. Benefits Uniqueness Way Forward First of its kind application in highwall mining in India
Integrates waste utilization with mine recovery Execution of this technique in the bottom seams and to improve the operational efficiency. Highwall Paste Filling in Quarry AB 2026
an Exhibition of Engineering Prowess

TE-026/077

title: VISION BASED HAUL ROAD SAFETY ALERT SYSTEM​ INTRODUCTION
description:
VISION BASED HAUL ROAD SAFETY ALERT SYSTEM​ INTRODUCTION
Delayed communication during unsafe events can lead to critical safety risks, especially in high‑risk environments like haul roads in open‑cast mines.
When warnings are not transmitted in real time, operators may unknowingly perform unsafe actions—resulting in collisions, equipment damage, or injuries.
This system uses advanced computer‑vision technology to continuously monitor haul‑road conditions and detect potential hazards in real time. PAIN AREA​ 
Poor road visibility
Berm disintegration or missing berms
Boulder presence on haul roads
Unauthorized light‑motor vehicles (LMV) entering HEMM lanes
Tailgating: Insufficient safe‑distance between heavy earth moving machinery (HEMM) CONVENTIONAL PROCESS
Manual intervention required: Daily manual-ticket logging after alerts received at the IRMSC department
Time delay in alert relay: ~7 hours


UNIQUENESS
Fully automated, real‑time, on‑board alert generation
Intelligent routing enables ultra‑fast alert relay
Alert generation time: 0.25 seconds PROPOSED SYSTEM​
Real‑time unsafe‑act detection using computer vision
In‑vehicle alerting via LoRa WAN connectivity
Centralized monitoring with visual alert dashboards
Reduced human error through automated detection
Smart Alert Management System for prioritization CHALLENGES​
Technical: Integration with FMS and managing false alerts
Behavioral: Operator resistance to frequent alert notifications (alert fatigue) BENEFITS​
Scalable and modular solution
Significant improvement in haul-road safety
Reduction in property damage and operational incidents
Early warnings reduce operator reaction time and improve decision making WAY FORWARD​
Pilot deployment in high‑risk zones
Data-driven optimization & model refinement
Expansion to additional hazard parameters and new mine areas 2026
an Exhibition of Engineering Prowess

TE-026/078

title: Automated Wagon Cleaning System Introduction: Proposed System:
description:
Automated Wagon Cleaning System Introduction: Proposed System:
The proposed solution introduces a vacuum‑based automated cleaning system to eliminate human entry into wagons and reduce man–machine interface. 
A high‑capacity suction unit removes coal, fines, dust and residual materials efficiently, supported by a cyclone separator and baghouse for dust control. 
It ensures safer, faster, and more consistent cleaning with minimal manual involvement. Benefits & Business Potential:
Automation significantly reduces injuries, cleaning time, manpower dependency, and wagon damage. 
Reduces the tare‑weight‑based demurrage, with estimated savings of ~₹6 lakh. 
Labour cost reduction can save over ₹1.24 crore annually. 
Improved throughput supports faster rake turnaround, enhancing dispatch reliability. The system’s modularity allows replication across RM locations. Future Scope:
The automated cleaning system can be deployed across multiple locations using standardised gantry frames, suction systems, and scrubbers. 
Future enhancements include IoT‑based monitoring, improved water recycling, energy‑efficient pumping, fully autonomous sequential wagon cleaning, and integration with yard‑level dispatch systems. 
Scalability supports BOXN, BOXNHL and similar wagon types. Challenges:
Integrating the proposed system  automatic camera detection requires wagon floor data which is insufficient as of now. The current wagon cleaning process is labor-intensive, unsafe , and heavily dependent on manual intervention, exposing workers to dust, fumes, slips, falls, and accidents. 
Injury reports include 31 First-Aid cases, 11 LTIs, and 1 fatality, highlighting significant risk. The manual cycle takes 40 minutes per wagon, causes operational delays, increases tare weight due to residual material, and leads to demurrage charges. 
The system also demands high manpower(10-12 workers per wagon), leading to annual labor costs exceeding ₹1.24 crore. Uniqueness:
The benefits of the project makes it the only feasible automated solution for low‑RL, water‑accumulated sites like Noamundi while significantly reducing injury risks and operational delays. 2026
an Exhibition of Engineering Prowess

TE-026/082

title: A Method for Extending the Service Life of Gas Conditioning Filters Through Non-Diesel Hydrocarbon featuring a Wash Oil Scrubbing Unit Introduction:
description:
A Method for Extending the Service Life of Gas Conditioning Filters Through Non-Diesel Hydrocarbon featuring a Wash Oil Scrubbing Unit Introduction:
Sludge, clogging, and corrosion cause unstable sampling and wrong CV readings.
Diesel scrubber is hazardous, causes corrosion and requires frequent maintenance.
Tar and naphthalene condensation raises pressure drop which leads to maintenance per shift. Proposed System:
Replace diesel with a wash oil scrubber.
Introduce a 4-stage filtration by adding a sintered metal filter.
In-house making of the whole filtration system to reduce dependence on the vendor.
Cuts corrosion, manual cleaning, and extends filter life. Benefits & Business Potential:
Environment-friendly, human-safe filtration system leading to less carbon emissions.
Less SRM & manpower, better ARC consolidation.
Less corrosion leading to longer shelf life.
Low-maintenance, fewer service calls.
Wash oil absorbs coal tar completely, unlike diesel which saturates quickly.
Wash oil is in-house produced; there is no capital involved necessarily.
Reuse of wash oil Future Scope:
3 & 4-stage systems with sintered housing made internally to cut costs.
Filter elements can be sourced from M/s Gopani Filters, Surat, for quality & reliability. Challenges:
In-house availability may fluctuate, but outsourced wash oil still remains cheaper than diesel. minimises overall waste generation. Newly Optimised Wash Oil Compared with Diesel Existing 3-Stage Filtration System – Diesel, Water, Ceramic Filter

title: Introduction:
description:
Introduction:
The door operates through a chain-tie rod system that undergoes high thermal and mechanical stress, causing tie-rod bending and fatigue.
This leads to failures at threading between chain joint and tie rod, and if the chain falls onto the roller table, causing major downtime.
A mechanism to prevent tie-rod fall and improve furnace door reliability is required. Proposed System:
A roller support frame arrangement mounted on an I-beam support directly above and below the chain path to arrest the chain movement- a fully mechanized solution.
When a break occurs, the chain joint gets mechanically trapped within the roller gap, preventing chain fall and reducing downtime by eliminating the additional steps like scaffolding, U-sealing, the need for furnace to cool. Benefits & Business Potential:
MTTR can be reduced by 6 hours.
Simplifies repair and maintenance process.
Financial savings per breakdown: 5–7 hrs × ₹15 lakhs/hr i.e.  ₹75 lakh to ₹1.05 crore saving per incident
Safety improvement: Zero scaffolding requirement, reduced furnace exposure, lower risk to manpower since we are preventing the issue of chain from falling on to the roller table.
Operational reliability: Planned maintenance to reduce downtime, stable production planning. Future Scope:
Incorporation of an auxiliary mechanism to hold gate in case of failure.
Integration of sensor, capable of sustaining high temp, in the vicinity of the most susceptible regions on chains. Challenges:
Pilot implementation 
Integrating the roller arrangement and ensuring precise placement in order. (Recorded data from September 2020 to June 2025) Furnace Door Reliability improvement in HSM Tie Rod Chain Joint Timeline of the breakdowns

title: PORTABLE COOLING SYSTEM FOR IR CAMERAS ​ . IR cameras on cranes help detect equipment overheating and improve visibility during operations.
description:
PORTABLE COOLING SYSTEM FOR IR CAMERAS ​ . IR cameras on cranes help detect equipment overheating and improve visibility during operations.
These cameras fail when exposed to temperatures beyond their safe operating limit.
The surrounding crane‑bay environment is much hotter than what the cameras can handle, leading to frequent malfunctions.
High heat causes unstable readings, reduced accuracy, and early camera failure.
A portable cooling system is needed to keep the cameras within a safe temperature range and ensure reliable performance 2026 Prevents overheating and improves camera reliability.
Enhances thermal monitoring accuracy and safety.
Extends camera lifespan and reduces replacement cost.
Portable design allows use on multiple cranes. Introduction Extremely high ambient temperatures around the crane.
Maintaining airflow in a compact portable system.
Preventing heat build-up inside the enclosure.
Ensuring reliability during continuous crane movement. Challenges Benefits First portable cooling system specially designed for crane‑mounted IR cameras.
Compact, cost‑effective, and easily deployable.
Combines insulation, heat sinking, and active cooling.
More efficient and lighter than commercial cooling housings. Uniqueness IR cameras operate directly in high‑temperature crane environments.
No cooling system is used to protect them from heat.
Cameras often exceed safe temperature limits and fail.
Breakdowns disrupt monitoring and increase maintenance cost. Conventional Process Block Diagram Proposed Solution Add enclosure to reduce heat exposure.
Use a heat sink with industrial fans for active cooling.
Use Temperature Gun for temperature Detection.
Design a portable unit for easy deployment on cranes. Standardize installation for all crane locations.
Expand deployment across all critical monitoring zones. Way Forward Power Supply Industrial Axial Fan Aluminum Heat sink IR camera Enclosure

title: Conversion of Plastic into fuel via Pyrolysis PROBLEM STATEMENT The Industrial Byproduct Management Department (IBMD) currently incurs costs by lending money to vendors for plastic waste and scrap disposal,  leading to an issue economically as well as environmentally. The current level of disposal of plastics by IBMD is 120 tons annually. PROPOSED UNIQUE SOLUTION Plastic is made up of multiple crosslinked hydrocarbon polymer chains.
description:
Conversion of Plastic into fuel via Pyrolysis PROBLEM STATEMENT The Industrial Byproduct Management Department (IBMD) currently incurs costs by lending money to vendors for plastic waste and scrap disposal,  leading to an issue economically as well as environmentally. The current level of disposal of plastics by IBMD is 120 tons annually. PROPOSED UNIQUE SOLUTION Plastic is made up of multiple crosslinked hydrocarbon polymer chains. 
Pyrolyzing these polymers at certain conditions could potentially break the linkages between them and produce hot polymer vapors at high temperature.
These polymers could be condensed to form fuel, which could then be used as energy source for various purposes. POTENTIAL BENEFITS No expenses or costs incurred to dispose plastic and  instead, the fuel produced could function as a  revenue generating platform.
Easily adapted to small-scale or large-scale applications according to the necessity.
Relatively implementable and feasible, as it involves no integration with other machines.
2 hrs of operation over 300 operating days in 500 kg capacity chamber can earn a revenue of 1.56 Cr /annum. FUTURE SCOPE Waste heat energy could be used to preheat the air and pass it through the heater of reactor for a better energy efficiency.
Fuel could be distilled for further classification of the oils in it.
Residual gases could be reused to heat the reactor whereas the black char could be laid along with the roads for pollution free disposal BLOCK DIAGRAM Pyrolyzing Cooling of gases in condenser Filtering of wax Plastic feeding Collection of oil CONVENTIONAL METHOD Currently IBMD give plastics to the vendor for the disposal and also, they lend money for disposal.

title: Condition monitoring of Pellet Car and Slide Track Business Impact
description:
Condition monitoring of Pellet Car and Slide Track Business Impact
Average downtime: 25–35 hours
Direct material loss due to 24 hours downtime: ₹8.1 crore Pellet Car Equipment B. Pellet equipment simulation Pain Points in Condition Monitoring
Slide track uprooting leads to plant breakdowns.
Unexpected equipment breakdowns had occurred.
Inspection is done manually through visual checks (no automation).
Excessive wear and tear reduces equipment life.
Difficult to identify which pellet car is defective Root cause analysis Proposed Solution Solution Benefits
Identification of defective pellet cars with exact location. Risk assessment of potential failure for each pellet car
Enables predictive maintenance and action prioritization
Eliminates manual visual inspection Real-time wheel-to-wheel distance monitoring using Ultrasonic sensors to detect clearance deviation and pellet car sagging. A. Pellet car The slide track and pellet car are critical parts of the induration machine in a pellet plant. It allows the pellet car to move smoothly and also helps prevent hot air from leaking from the induration zone during operation. Introduction

title: Semi Automated Roll Grinder For Roughing Stands In Wire Rod Mill . In Wire Rod Mill, the incoming hot billet is passed on to the consecutive stands by the rolls of the Roughing stand.
description:
Semi Automated Roll Grinder For Roughing Stands In Wire Rod Mill . In Wire Rod Mill, the incoming hot billet is passed on to the consecutive stands by the rolls of the Roughing stand.
It is important for the rolls of the Roughing stand to have a firm grip on the billet as there is no other mechanism for pushing the hot billet into the consecutive stands.
Therefore the surface of the roll needs to have enough friction/roughness. This roughness is created by grinding the rolls using abrasive material.
Over time this roughness is lost due to wear and tear of the roll as it processes the hot billets. This smoothens the surface and grip/bite of the roll is lost. So the process of grinding needs to be repeated periodically. 2026 Selection of motion system, control method and grinding tools Design and Simulation of robot arm in cad Integration of control system and sensors Define objective, specifications and precision of the robotic arm Creation of working prototype and testing and tuning Implement the robotic arm in WRM Elimination of man machine interference thereby removing safety risk.
Reducing roll grinding time and thus downtime.
Accurate tracking and adjustment of gridning tool speed, force and depth of grinding.
Increasing dimensional accuracy thus ensuring consistent friction across all rolls.
Increasing roll lifespan by minimizing the grinding depth to just the required amount. Introduction Ensuring the robotic arm is compact lightweight and portable and detachable.
Providing smooth and jitter free motion of the motors via the controller. 
Ensuring proper calibration and accuracy of the sensors Challenges Benefits The robotic arm will be developed with flexible and modular hardware so that it can adapted to various mills wherever grinding is required. The tools and sensors can be made to be interchangeable and any profile can be traced via firmware variations. Uniqueness The roll surface is roughened manually using a handheld grinding tool.
One worker grinds away at the roll surface while another rotates the roll.
Manual intervention is involved during the grinding and poses a safety risk as the workers need to do their job near heavy rotating machinery. Conventional Process Block Diagram Proposed Solution Development of a semi automatic robotic arm for grinding the rolls of the roughing stand.
The robotic arm has 3 Degrees of Freedom(DoF) in the linear axes(X,Y and Z). This linear motions will be used for moving across the roll passes, shifting the grinding tool from top roll to bottom roll and either moving the tool towards the roll or retracting it.
The robotic arm also has a servo arm that has 3 DoF in the rotational axes and then it has a swinging mechanism at the end of the servo arm to hold the grinding tool and apply pressure on the roll using adjustable springs. There are force sensors to accurately measure the force applied while grinding and high precision piezoelectric film sensor for  measuring the grinding depth. Refining and increasing the robustness of the design and polishing the control system.
Testing and tuning the robotic arm in the working environment.
Implementation of the robotic arm in Wire Rod Mill. Way Forward

title: Benefits
description:
Benefits
Improves combustion efficiency, resulting in approximately 4–8% fuel savings.
Helps reduce air pollution, supports pollution‑control efforts, and contributes to climate‑change mitigation.
Quick to install and requires no major modifications to existing equipment. Magnets break large clusters into smaller groups.
FIR ceramics chamber heat/excite that mist, so it ignites instantly.
Copper/Electrostatic forces cause those groups to repel each other into a fine mist. Introduction 
All thermal utilities generate heat via combustion.
Combustion efficiency depends on fuel atomization and air-fuel mixing. Business potential
Fuel Savings: 3-5%
Quick Installation & Payback
No Major System Modifications Challenges
Performance varies by fuel type.
Requires precise sizing & placement.
Needs baseline fuel data for validation. Way forward
Can be used in any plant where combustion processes are involved. MAGNETIC RESONATOR-BASED FUEL MANAGEMENT TECHNOLOGY Enhancing combustion efficiency| Reducing Fuel Consumption | Lowering emission of CO2 Proposed system Block diagram Carbon footprint stats
For every 1 Liter per Metric Ton (L/Mt) improvement in fuel efficiency, you save approximately:
3.1 kg of CO2​ for every ton of product produced. MAGNETIC RESONATOR-BASED FUEL MANAGEMENT TECHNOLOGY

title: Benifits/ Business Potantial Improves strip stability, enabling higher looper utilization (>50%) for wider coils (width > 1400 mm).
description:
Benifits/ Business Potantial Improves strip stability, enabling higher looper utilization (>50%) for wider coils (width > 1400 mm).

Minimizes strip damage, resulting in significant cost savings across the supply chain. Active Strip Sway Correction at Entry Looper Introduction & Problem Statement The Entry Looper of PLTCM, CRM TSJ ensures continuous strip flow between the entry section and pickling section by maintaining a buffer that compensates for speed differences.

The tension imbalance at the entry looper lead to strip shifting and swaying.

Current practice involves slowing down of line on the basis of the output given by Laser Distance Meter (LDM) sensors installed on the looper car. Proposed Solution Losses Incurred Block Diagram Way Forward and Challenges Implement the proposed active strip sway‑correction system at CRM‑TSJ on the entry looper and monitor its impact on strip stability and looper utilization.

Implementation of this solution will require a temporary plant shutdown and a significant capital investment. / We propose a modification in the looper car frame design by extending the looper car into a ‘Hydraulic Car’ that accommodates a hydraulic power pack to actuate Roll #3. LDM sensor output θ (theta) : Yaw angle 
V : Speed of the sheet over the roll (mm/min) 
L : Length of the roll
F : Force of actuation
M : Mass of the roll
D : Diameter of the roll
x : Deviation from center in mm

title: SMART RF-BASED REMOTE TRACK SWITCHING SYSTEM FOR SAFE AND EFFICIENT TORPEDO MOVEMENT AT HML . PROBLEM STATEMENT
description:
SMART RF-BASED REMOTE TRACK SWITCHING SYSTEM FOR SAFE AND EFFICIENT TORPEDO MOVEMENT AT HML . PROBLEM STATEMENT
The plant has 165 track switching points. The torpedo ladle lead time to the LD shops is approximately 45 minutes.
There are 120 daily trips using 42 torpedoes (organized into 21 rakes). Each rake completes an average of 2.8 trips per day.
Each trip passes through 7–8 switching points, and manual switching results in approximately 17 minutes of idle time per trip.
Ground crew members must dismount to change track points, creating exposure to movement-related risks.
Level‑crossing safety is managed manually by the ground crew.
There are approximately 11 vulnerable areas with unbarricaded level crossings. FATALITY INCIDENT
A fatality incident of a ground crew  has happened at HML during manual operation at night.(Late G C Verma ,P. No 114895)
RECORDED RECENT STF CASES
On 25-Apr-2023, IP-1slipped while stepping down from loco, injuring his right ankle. (IN00164001)
On 15-Feb-2024, IP-2 fell while climbing stairs of loco#65. (IN00184517))
On 18-Aug-2024, IP-3 slipped and fell down near the track while going to switch the point#19. (IN00198337)) Fig.1: Ground crew manually flagging loco carrying hot metal torpedo at un-barricaded railway crossing Fig. 2: Manually operated track switching panel PROPOSED SYSTEM
AUTOMATED DROP-GATE SYSTEM (PRE-REQUISITE):
Robust and reliable IP68 rated proximity sensor that will trigger alarm and drop-gate in uncrowded areas.

SMART REMOTE FOR TRACK SWITCHING:
Enables remote operation of track switches without requiring the locomotive to stop.
The system comprises an RF transceiver at both the remote unit and the switch point, an Arduino UNO, an OLED display on the remote, and a pushbutton interface for switch activation.
Includes an LED indicator to inform the locomotive operator of the current track alignment.
Uses the NRF24L01+ module with unique ID assignment for each receiver, enabling independent and interference‑free control of multiple switch points.
Incorporates fool‑proof addressing to prevent incorrect switching due to proximity or overlapping signals.
Programming involves interfacing the RF module with an Arduino board and developing the control logic using the RF24 library within the Arduino IDE environment. CHALLENGES
Requires proper mounting of the transceiver circuit, as the system may be susceptible to packet loss.
Sensors must be protected against dust, water, and heat to ensure reliable operation.
Requires repeated and thorough testing to eliminate any possibility of derailment. BENEFITS
Ground crew safety will improve, with a corresponding reduction in Slip–Trip–Fall cases.
Manpower requirement will reduce by approximately 46%.
Halt time per rake will reduce by about 17 minutes per trip.
Turnaround efficiency will increase, improving from 2.9 to 3.2 trips per rake.
Refractory lining life will increase.
Production capacity will increase, with an estimated additional 2,500 tons of hot metal throughput (calculated as 320 × 38 × 0.2) using the existing infrastructure. UNIQUENESS
Indigenously developed RF‑based remote track‑switching.
Ensures real‑time, secure signal transmission with fail‑safe interlocking for safe switching operations.
Designed to be scalable and fully compatible with the existing yard infrastructure.
Provides digital logging of operations for enhanced safety, audit traceability, and predictive maintenance. FUTURE SCOPE
Mesh networking of wireless nodes for enhanced coverage and redundancy.
Remote monitoring with seamless SCADA integration for centralized control.
Predictive maintenance analytics for improved reliability and reduced downtime.

title: Introduction & Problem Statement
description:
Introduction & Problem Statement
Skip‑hoist systems and rail logistics in lime plants, blast furnaces, and excavated pits serve as essential production lifelines.
Tracks operate at steep inclines (high‑angle) and are subjected to heavy impact loads.
Derailments occur due to a combination of wheel slippage, material spillage, rail wear, and joint defects.
Current maintenance practices rely on fixed‑interval replacement, leading to capital waste (premature scrapping of healthy tracks) or unplanned downtime (unexpected failures).
Manual inspection in high‑angle or hazardous zones creates a critical man–machine interface (MMI) risk. TRACK GEOMETRY MONITORING SYSTEM (Focus: Lime Plant, Blast Furnace & Industrial Rail Logistics) Proposed Solution
 A single Sensor Car is used to detect all track-related defects.
Two sensor categories are used: Ultrasonic and IMU Sensors
Ultrasonic sensors measure track gauge and detect widening or rail mushrooming.
IMU sensor monitors cross‑level, alignment, and vibration to identify track defects. The Reality: Industrial Scalability
Scaling requires the Sensor Car to maintain consistent accuracy across extended track sections.
Seamless integration with existing skip or loco operations is critical for effective large‑scale deployment.
Wider deployment increases the need for regular sensor calibration and robust maintenance planning.
Large‑scale implementation produces substantial data volumes, necessitating efficient processing and reliable defect‑reporting systems. Versatility & Application
Hazardous Zones: Designed for deep excavation pits and underground mines where human access is legally restricted.
Remote Safety: Wireless data transmission enables operators to assess track health from a safe control room, minimizing the need for “Red Zone” entry. Benefits
Minimizes MMI Risk: Significantly reduces the need for manual inspection on hazardous inclines, lowering the potential for accidents.
Asset Life Extension: Enables a shift from time‑based to condition‑based maintenance, resulting in substantial Capex savings.
Production Gain: Prevents unplanned derailments (“furnace starvation”) and reduces slippage‑related wear. Way Forward
Pilot industrial validation at the plant.
Integrate with the plant‑wide monitoring system.
Deploy and standardize for full‑scale adoption. Uniqueness
Compact retrofittable sensor module.
Real-time geometry analytics dashboard.
Scalable and adaptable to different track systems.

title: Bifacial solar panels are installed as floating units on the upper cooling pond.
description:
Bifacial solar panels are installed as floating units on the upper cooling pond.
They generate power from direct sunlight on the top surface and reflected light on the bottom surface.
Oil present in the pond adheres to the lower surface of the panels, blocking reflected light.
This fouling reduces panel efficiency to about 12%, compared with the global average of around 25%. Inspired By Tata Steel 2045 Sustainability Goal Smart Oil Skimming System for Solar Water Installation Benefits & Business Potential ENERGY PERFORMANCE
Efficiency Gain: +5% 
Monthly Output: +50 MWH Introduction & Problem Statement Solution : 1.Installing Oil Booms
Oil booms are the floating barriers installed around solar panels to contain surface oil.
They prevent oil from spreading and reaching the lower surface of panels. 2. Autonomous Oil Skimmer boat
Self operating boat designed to remove surface oil.
Rotating belt made of oleophilic material moves through the water surface.
Oil adheres to the belt and carried upward.
Collected oil is scrapped into storage container for safe disposal. REVENUE GENERATION

title: Smart TLC Decoupling Detection and Safety Interlock Introduction
description:
Smart TLC Decoupling Detection and Safety Interlock Introduction
Currently, coupler lock engagement between Torpedo Ladle Cars (TLC) and locomotives is verified manually, often in conditions with dust, fumes, or low visibility.
This manual verification increases the risk of errors, potentially leading to decoupling failures and roll‑down accidents.

Proposed System
Laser and LDR module senses and indicates the alignment status of the coupler.
Proximity sensor confirms physical lock engagement.
Automated safety interlock operated through a solenoid actuator.
Powered by a battery that can self‑recharge via an axle‑driven dynamometer. Benefits
Eliminates human dependency and manual errors by providing active barriers for reliable coupling.
Prevents roll‑down and derailment incidents, addressing a red‑risk process hazard.
Self‑powered design with minimal infrastructure requirements.

Way Forward
Horizontal deployment across all locations.
Can become a standard safety interlock for rail‑based logistics.

Challenges
Maintaining sensor efficiency and accuracy under high vibration and dusty conditions. Block diagram

title: Smart Sensor-based System to Prevent Steel Car Derailment in the Steel Plant Introduction:
description:
Smart Sensor-based System to Prevent Steel Car Derailment in the Steel Plant Introduction:
Steel Plant faces steel/slag car derailments caused by slag jamming on the track or wheel imbalance.
This year in LD#1, steel car derailments occurred twice, each causing a delay of nearly 24 hours.
Each vessel taps approximately 30 heats in 24 hours, resulting in an opportunity loss of 30 heats per derailment. Proposed System:
Install smart tilt sensors on the steel car bogies.
An embedded controller continuously monitors sensor inputs using threshold‑based anomaly detection.
The system sends an alert or triggers an auto‑stop command before a derailment can occur. Benefits & Business Potential:
Prevents ₹2.40 crore loss per derailment (₹4.80 crore in two incidents) 
Reduces 24-hour production downtime from each derailment.
Prevents molten metal accidents & improves worker safety
Auto stop before derailment Future Scope:
Potential to integrate with IoT and AI systems for predicting and detecting slag accumulation on the track, advancing the system beyond emergency stopping.
Scalability: The solution can be rapidly deployed across all LD shops and other steel plant units. Challenges:
High temperature and dust area. BLOCK DIAGRAM Installation of sensors on car Derailment risk detection algorithm Alert/Auto Stop System Monitoring (LD Control Room ) 2026 Tilt Sensor Dashboard Prototype
